content.full_text:
NAPA COUNTY                                 OFFICE OF COUNTY COUNSEL

1195 THIRD STREET, ROOM 301 • NAPA, CALIFORNIA 94559-3001
AREA CODE 707/253-4521 FAX 707/253-4176

ROBERT WESTMEYER, County Counsel

December 17, 1991

MARGARET L. WOODBURY, Chief Deputy
JOSEPH C. FOLKARD, Deputy
DAVID L. ZALTSMAN, Deputy                          Our File No. 1410.500
KEVIN C. STOBAUGH, Deputy
JAYNE F. HERMAN, Deputy


Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
Attn: Legal Division

Ladies and Gentlemen:

Kathryn J. Winter, Chairperson of the Napa County Conservation, Development and
Planning Commission (the "Commission"), hereby requests a written opinion from
your office as to whether a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act
exists based upon the following facts:

Chairperson Winter's husband, Mick Winter, is currently employed in a non-
management position as a systems analyst by The Doctors' Company, a physician-
owned nonprofit medical malpractice insurer ("Doctors"). Doctors, a company
with annual revenues of approximately one hundred fifty million dollars, has
offices at 1127 First Street, Napa in Napa County. Among it's many assets,
Doctors possesses approximately 11 acres of land in the unincorporated portion
of Napa County, including the approximately five-acre site on which its new
offices are being constructed and approximately six acres of undeveloped land.
Doctors has no current plans to develop or sell its undeveloped land but may, in
the future, change its position as to these holdings.

The Housing Element of the Napa County General Plan states that the provision of
affordable housing in the County is to be encouraged in connection with the
development of residential projects. The Commission is currently scheduled to
consider whether to recommend to the Napa County Board of Supervisors (the
"Board") that an inclusionary housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part
of a comprehensive response to this aspect of the County's General Plan. Such a
recommendation, if made, could include endorsement of a model ordinance
requiring some or all of the following: that a certain percentage of all new
residential units constructed in a project be "affordable" as that term is
defined in the ordinance; that a portion of affordable housing fund in
lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or that construction of a
certain number of affordable units take place off-site. In addition, the
Commission is likely to recommend the creation of additional sources of funding
for affordable housing through means such as assessment of linkage fees from
developers of agricultural, commercial and industrial projects based upon

December 17, 1991
Page 2

project-generated employment, imposition of a business license tax or increases
in documentary transfer or sales taxes. Should Doctors ever decide to develop
its undeveloped land in unincorporated Napa County, it would be subject to any
ordinance ultimately adopted by the Board.

The State Department of Housing and Community Development has required that some
form of inclusionary program be referred to the Board by December 31, 1991.
Failure to do so may reduce or eliminate the ability of the County to qualify
for Community Development Block Grant funds. We would therefore very much
appreciate expedited handling of this request and the return of a written
opinion to this office by December 26, 1991, if possible, so that a special
meeting of the Commission may be held on December 30, 1991. If you have any
questions as you conduct your review, please do not hesitate to contact either
me (707/253-4521) or Chairperson Winter (707/252-6222 or 707/257-2737). Thank
you for your attention to this matter.

Very truly yours,

JAMES F. HERMAN
Deputy County Counsel

cc: Kathryn J. Winter
Paul Battisti

[2000/request.opw]

embedding.qa_text: This letter is from the Office of County Counsel for Napa County, California, dated December 17, 1991. It is addressed to the Fair Political Practices Commission and concerns a written opinion regarding a potential conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act. The letter states: --- ROBERT WESTMEYER, County Counsel MARGARET L. WOODBURY, Chief Deputy JOSEPH C. FOLKARD, Deputy DAVID L. ZALTSMAN, Deputy KEVIN C. STOBAUGH, Deputy JAYNE F. HERMAN, Deputy December 17, 1991 Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Attn: Legal Division Ladies and Gentlemen: Kathryn J. Winter, Chairperson of the Napa County Conservation, Development and Planning Commission (the "Commission"), hereby requests a written opinion from your office as to whether a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act exists based upon the following facts: Chairperson Winter's husband, Mick Winter, is currently employed in a non-management position as a systems analyst by The Doctors' Company, a physician-owned nonprofit medical malpractice insurer ("Doctors"). Doctors, a company with annual revenues of approximately one hundred fifty million dollars, has offices at 1127 First Street, Napa in Napa County. Among its many assets, Doctors possesses approximately 11 acres of land in the unincorporated portion of Napa County, including the approximately five-acre site on which its new offices are being constructed and approximately six acres of undeveloped land. Doctors has no current plans to develop or sell its undeveloped land but may, in the future, change its position as to these holdings. The Housing Element of the Napa County General Plan states that the provision of affordable housing in the County is to be encouraged in connection with the development of residential projects. The Commission is currently scheduled to consider whether to recommend to the Napa County Board of Supervisors (the "Board") that an inclusionary housing ordinance be developed and adopted as part of a comprehensive response to this aspect of the County's General Plan. Such a recommendation, if made, could include endorsement of a model ordinance requiring some or all of the following: that a certain percentage of all new residential units constructed in a project be "affordable" as that term is defined in the ordinance; that a fee be paid to an affordable housing fund in lieu of the actual construction of affordable units; or that construction of a certain number of affordable units take place off-site. In addition, the Commission is likely to recommend the creation of additional sources of funding for affordable housing through means such as assessment of linkage fees from developers of agricultural, commercial and industrial projects based upon December 17, 1991 Page 2 project-generated employment, imposition of a business license tax or increases in documentary transfer or sales taxes. Should Doctors ever decide to develop its undeveloped land in unincorporated Napa County, it would be subject to any ordinance ultimately adopted by the Board. The State Department of Housing and Community Development has required that some form of inclusionary program be referred to the Board by December 31, 1991. Failure
